coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted fabrication of La/Cu/Zr/carbon dots trimetallic nanocomposites with their adsorptional vs photocatalytic efficiency for remediation of persistent organic pollutants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Gaurav
Bhogal, Sangeeta
Naushad, Mu.
Inamuddin,
Kumar, Amit
Stadler, Florian J.
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report a greener microwave method for fabrication of La/Cu/Zr/carbon quantum dots trimetallic nanocomposites(La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs). The structural aspects and chemical nature of the TNCs were considered using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The optical band property of the trimetallic nanocomposites was examined using UV–vis spectroscopy. The adsorptional versus photocatalytic activity of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was investigated for the removal of a persistent organic pollutant i.e., malachite green dye and ampicillin antibiotic, under visible light irradiation. Overall, the current approach relates to green chemistry principles for fabrication La/Cu/Zr/CQDs trimetallic nanocomposites which hold promise for the development of surprisingly proficient adsorptional-photocatalytic systems. It has been observed that 96% ampicillin antibiotic and 86% of malachite green were degraded in 4h of photoirridation under adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Trimetallic nanocompositesadsorptional/photocatalytic activity
Visible light
Malachite green
Ampicillin antibiotic
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Band gap studies of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs
Remediation of persistent organic pollutants using La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Shows the structure of Ampicillin antibiotic (a) and (b) Malachite Green dye. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM photographs of synthesized La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs (a and b) and TEM photographs of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs (c–d).
Tauc plot for CQDs (a) and La/Cu/Zr/CQDs trimetallic nanocomposites(b).

Spectra of ampicillin antibiotic: (a) adsorptional in dark followed by photocatalysis and (b) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis; percentage degradation of Ampicillin: (c) adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and (d) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis; psedo-firest-order kinetics for degradation of ampicillin: (e) adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and (f) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis in presence of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs.

Spectra of MG: (a) adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and (b) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis; percentage degradation of MG: (c) adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and (d) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis; pseudo-first-order kinetics for photodegradation of MG (e) adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and (f) coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis in presence of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs.
d-spacing values of the prepared La/Cu/Zr/CQDs trimetallic nanocomposites.

% Removal of Ampicillin antibiotic and Malachite Green dye by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs under two reaction conditions: equilibrium adsorption followed by photocatalysis and coupled adsorption/photocatalysis.
Apparent rate constants and linear regression coefficients from log A0/At vs T plots for Ampicillin and MG dye.

row.entry: Adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted fabrication of La/Cu/Zr/carbon dots trimetallic nanocomposites with their adsorptional vs photocatalytic efficiency for remediation of persistent organic pollutants

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry, Shoolini University, Solan 173212, Himachal Pradesh, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report a greener microwave method for fabrication of La/Cu/Zr/carbon quantum dots trimetallic nanocomposites(La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs). The structural aspects and chemical nature of the TNCs were considered using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The optical band property of the trimetallic nanocomposites was examined using UV–vis spectroscopy. The adsorptional versus photocatalytic activity of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was investigated for the removal of a persistent organic pollutant i.e., malachite green dye and ampicillin antibiotic, under visible light irradiation. Overall, the current approach relates to green chemistry principles for fabrication La/Cu/Zr/CQDs trimetallic nanocomposites which hold promise for the development of surprisingly proficient adsorptional-photocatalytic systems. It has been observed that 96% ampicillin antibiotic and 86% of malachite green were degraded in 4h of photoirridation under adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis process.

ce:section.ce:para: The last few decades has witnessed an enormous curiosity in the creation of carbon quantum dots and their applications in a variety of fields due to their simplicity of creation and functionalization and, their exceptional properties such as good biocompatibility and size [1–4]. Recently, a new class of discovered nanocarbons i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs) had better redox properties, luminescence, high stability and photo induced electron transfer [5,6]. CQDs are typically encrusted with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which can really boost their biocompatibility. CQDs are low molecular weight and nontoxic in nature, which makes them better over other quantum dots [1,7]. This leads to expansion in applications of CQDs in numerous areas, such as photocatalysis [8,9], fluorescent sensor [10], peroxidase-like catalysts for the colorimetric detection of glucose [11,12] and chemical imaging etc. Rising environmental issues have made the researchers to discover some particles or materials that are not harmful to nature. CQDs can be fabricated by using diverse methods that can be generally classified into “top down” and “bottom up” routes. Top down methods relates to “breaking” of big carbon structures into smaller minor matter, where as the bottom up route rely on CQDs creation from simpler molecular entities. The bottom up route has the advantage of sustainability with green chemistry principles. Amongst these methods, chemical or thermal oxidation of molecular entities represents an appealing way to create CQDs in huge quantity. Carbon- based quantum dots (CQDs, C-dots or CDs) offer an alternative to the conventionally used toxic materials. CQDs were first accidently exposed during the refinement of single-walled carbon nanotubes which were prepared through electrophoresis. Later on, these were also prepared through laser ablation of graphite powder and cement in 2006 [13–15]. In addition, CQDs are also abundant and inexpensive in nature which increases their utility range [16]. Carbon is principally a black material, and normally deliberated to have low solubility in water and feeble fluorescence. Extensive consideration has been concentrated on CQDs due of their strong luminescence; that is why they are also referred to as carbon nanolights [17].

Integration of carbon with inorganic functional counterparts represents a healthy approach for budding new functionalities and enhancing their properties. Consequently, techniques for fabrication of composite materials with inorganic and organic components incorporated in precise architectures are of notable importance and might cover a wider approach for selection of new professional materials. Combination of CQDs with dissimilar materials such as ZnO, TiO2 Ag3PO4, SiO2, Fe2O3, Cu2O and CdS has been studied. The finer biological properties of carbon quantum dots, assign them with probable role in bioimaging, drug delivery and biosensor etc. [18]. The prominent electronic properties of CQDs as electron acceptor and donor, which is reason for chemiluminescence and electrochemical luminescence behaviour of CQDs, bestow them with ample potentials in optronics, catalysis and sensors etc. Trimetallics nanoparticles are advance class of nanomaterials gaining a much more attention in recent times because of their better catalytic properties. For the diverse applications, a variety of fabrication routes have been reported for the trimetallic nanoparticles. It has been revealed that in metal catalysts, adding up of other metal can improve the catalytic nature and selectivity of the catalyst, thus revolution from momometallic to bimetallic and trimetallic nanoparticles catalyst has been observed in recent times. Trimetallic nanoparticles have an extra degree of catalytic activity compared to the bimetallic and monometallic nanoparticles. But entire catalytic nature of trimetallics depends upon their constituent’s metals and their compositions, only little information of trimetallic nanoparticles in the literature is available. Hence it is well established that the synergistic amalgamation of dissimilar metals in bimetallics (BM) and trimetallics (TM) nanoparticles (NPs) appreciably change the catalytic properties of the constituent’s metals. The trimetallic nanoparticles were found to be efficient catalyst for various applications [19–21].

The application of trimetallicas and their efficient composites as photocatalyst for remediation of pollutants is still need to be explored. The considerable characteristics of the photocatalytic material are low band gap, larger surface area, better stability and higher reusability etc. The diverse metal oxides such as CuO, NiO, ZnO, Ag2O, TiO2, CeO2, ZrO2 and Bi2O3, oxychloride BiOCl and nanocomposites has been investigated as photocalytic materials. The irradiation of these characteristics photocatalysts induces a charge separation process with the formation of electrons and positive holes that are able to oxidize organic pollutants. In this paper, the CQDs modified La/Cu/Zr trimetallic naoparticles is prepared by greener microwave method. Further, the structure of the trimetallic nanocomposites and the mechanism enhancement for the exclusion of persistent organic pollutants was investigated. The results revealed that the TNCs were energetic under visible light irradiation and their effectiveness was much higher.

All chemicals used in this investigation were analytical reagent grade and used as received. Na2C6H5O7·2H2O (S.D Fine Chem Limited), ZrOCl2·8H2O (LobaChemiePvt. Ltd), La (NO3)3·6H2O (Central Drug house Pvt. Ltd) and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (Thermo electron LLS India Pvt. Ltd). De-ionized water was used throughout the experimental work. X-Ray diffractrometer(Perkin Elmer Series IICHNS/O 2400), Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum- BX USA), UV–vis spectrophotometer (Systeronics), Scanning electron microscope (Quanta 250, FEI Make Mode No. D9393) and Transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai F 20).

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) aqueous suspension was made by mixing 4mL d-fructose (500mM) and 4mL of NaOH (500mM) [22]. The suspension was heated at 100W for 5min in microwave oven to obtained carbon quantum dots. La/Cu/Zr/CQDs nanostructures were fabricated by a greener microwave technique. 20mL of 20mM lanthanum nitrate and 3mL of 2% trisodium citrate solution were mixed in the formerly ready CQDs suspension at room temperature. The reaction mixture was kept in microwave oven at 400W for 2min in cyclic mode (10s ON 5s OFF) to avoid intense heating. After that in above mixture 20mL of 20mM cupric nitrate solution was added followed by addition of 3mL of 2% trisodium citrate. Again the reaction mixture was kept in microwave oven for 2min at cyclic mode as discussed above. Then 20mL of 40mM of zirconium oxychloride solution and 3mL of 2% trisodium citrate was added to reaction mixture andagain kept in microwave in cyclic mode for 2min. Finally, obtained dark brown colour precipitates of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNPs were filtered and washed numerous times to eliminate impurities. The obtained precipitate was oven dried up at 50°C for 1h.

Fourier transform infrared spectrum of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNPs were taken by KBr disc method. Dried La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNPs (10mg) were mixed with KBr powder (100mg) in an agate mortar. The mixture was hard-pressed into a pellet under 10 tons load for 4min, and the spectrum was recorded instantaneously. Sixteen accumulative scans were taken. The signal from a pure KBr pellet was removed as the background. FTIR absorptionspectrum was recorded in the region of 400–4000cm−1. The X-Ray Diffraction pattern of the trimetallic nanocomposite was recorded with an analytical diffractrometer using Cu Kαradiation (k=1.5418Å).

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical’s X'Pert Pro) analysis was done out in reflectance mode. X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded from 5° to 90°θ for La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs. The particle size is investigated by applying Scherrer’s formula [23,24]:
(1)
p =kλ/βCosθ
Where p =particle size, β =full width at half maximum, λ =1.54Ǻ for Cu Kα radiation and K =0.9. For wurtzite structure, the lattice constants are calculated by using the formula as [25]:
(2)
1
d hkl 2
=
4 3
h 2
+ hk +
k 2
a 2
+
l 2
c 2

For analyzing the prepared La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs by SEM (scanning electron microscope JSM- 6100), excellent powders of sample was employed to synthesize grids. The SEM images were captured at different magnifications. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) examination was done on a Technai G2 20 S-TWIN at acceleration voltage 20–200kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap studies of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis spectrophotometer (Systeronics-2205) was used for primary characterization of prepared La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs. For obtaining out the band gap, a dispersion of 0.5mg/mL La/Cu/Zr/CQDsTNCsand CQDs were made in ethanol. The UV–vis spectra of the two were taken employing spectrophotometer (range from 300 to 800nm). By using Tauc’s relation [26,27] the optical band gap were calculated from absorption coefficient data.
(3) αhv =B(hv−Eg ) n
Where α is absorption coefficient, hν is photon energy, Eg is optical band gap of the nanoparticle, B is band tailing parameter, n =2 for indirect band gap and n =1/2 for direct band gap. The absorption coefficient [α], at the equivalent wavelengths, was calculated by using the Beer–Lambert’s relation:
(4)
α =
2.303 A
l
Where, l is path length and A is absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Remediation of persistent organic pollutants using La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
The persistent organic pollutants (malachite green and ampicillin antibiotic) removal was investigated using La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs. Fig. 1 shows the structure of two pollutants under study; (a) ampicillin (b) malachite green. The adsorptional/photocatalysis study was performed in a thermally insulated double walled Pyrex glass chamber to maintain the steady temperature [28,29]. For controlled agitation a magnetic stirrer was used. 100mg of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs were added into the solution of malachite green dye (2 × 10−6 molL−1) and ampicillin antibiotic (50ppm). To study the adsorption versus photocatalysis and coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis processes together the one set was placed in dark to achieve the adsorption – desorption equilibrium and the other was kept directly in solar radiation for coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis [30]. On establishment of the equilibrium after keeping in dark, the solution was further uncovered to sunlight with intermittent agitation for photocatalysis. An aliquot of 3mL was withdrawn at different intervals of time, centrifuged to eliminate the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs particles and absorbance was recorded. The removal process kinetics for MG and ampicillin were studied at 620nm and 216nm. The percent removal of dye and antibiotic were calculated as [31].
(5)
R e m o v a l ( % ) =
A
o
− A
A o
× 100
Where, Ao and A are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of dye and ampicillin. The removal rate of MG and antibiotic was calculated using pseudo-first-order kinetics model as [32]:
(6)
r = −
d A
d t
=
k
a p p
t

The above equation can also be represented as:
(7) In Ao/A= kappt
Where kapp
=apparent rate constant, Ao =concentration of dye and antibiotic before illumination and A=concentration of dye and antibiotic at time t (minute).

The FTIR spectrum of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs in Fig. 2 shows the symmetric and asymmetric CH stretching vibration peak at 2849cm−1and 2921cm−1 hence confirm the existence of CQDs in La/Cu/Zr TNCs. The characteristic peak present at 1062cm−1 assigned to CC stretching. The FTIR spectrum of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs depicts a broad peak of OH stretching vibration mode at 3392cm−1 [33]. Additional peak at 1416cm−1 relates to CO vibrations of oxygen containing groups. The peaks were observed for ZrO at 580cm−1, for CuO at 481cm−1 and for LaO at 721cm−1 [29,34,35]. Confirming the formation of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD-diffraction pattern of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs. The figure exhibits that no characteristic peaks for CQDs in the XRD patterns of the TNCs because of their non-crystalline nature and little content [22]. The spectra revealed that CQDs addition had no noticeable change on purity and crystallinity of the La/Cu/Zr particles. Lanthanum shows the peaks at 2θ angle values at 27.40° and 30.26° which corresponds to (002) and (111) diffraction planes (JCPDS no. 02-0607) [36]. Copper exhibit the peaks at 2θ angle values of 43° and 47° corresponding to (111) and (220) diffraction planes (JCPDS no. 04-0784)[37]. The figure shows the peaks at 2θ angles at 30.17° and 60.03° corresponding to (101) and (211) diffraction planes with an excellent match with the standard JCPDS file no. 81-1544 [38] for zirconium. The corresponding values of 2θ and their planes are given in Table 1 . The average crystalline size for La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs determined from strongest diffraction peaks using scherrer’s formula is 59.96nm. The lattice constant for hexagonal lanthanum is a=6.129Å3 and c=2.421Å3, for face centred cube copper are as follow a=b=c=2.59Å3 and for tetragonal zirconium is a=b=1.43Å3 and c=1.17Å3.

The SEM images of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs were shown in Fig. 4 (a and b) decapitates cluster based framework of CQDs attached to La/Cu/Zr particles, having fibrous morphology confirming binding of trimetallics to CQDs. TEM present the information about the particle size of the material. TEM images of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs shown in Fig. 4(c and d) at various magnification indicates that the particle size lies in the range of 30–100nm for La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs.

UV–vis spectrum of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs shows an absorption band in between 300 and 490nm. The absorption edge of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs within the visible light around 490nm corresponding to the band gap 1.56eV. The Tauc plots for direct band gap were resolved using the Tauc relation.
The optical band gap was found out by extrapolating the straight portion of the curve involving [αhv]2 and hv when α=0. The tauc plots for CQDs and La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs are given in Fig. 5 .

The optical band gap for CQDs was 2.55eV laying in the semiconductor region, The band gap of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was 1.56eV which lies nearer to semiconductors thus can assist absorption in the visible region.

The adsorptional-photocatalytic activity of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was carried out by studying the removal of textile dye and antibiotic under natural sunlight. For adsorptional-photocatalytic studies malachite green and ampicillin antibiotic were used as test pollutants. Their removal was simply monitored by optical absorption spectroscopy.

The basic mechanism of removal is composed of two processes i.e, adsorption and photocatalysis. The adsorption of dye and antibiotic occurs on the surface of TNCs. Photocatalysis process involves production of electron-hole pairs on irradiation of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs with light,which can react with water molecules to give up hydroxyl and super oxide radicals leading to the mineralization of the antibiotic and dye molecules [39]. Since, the band gap of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was only 1.56eV. It can easily be excited by the solar light to generate electron-hole pairs.MG is a cationic dye and it requires electrons from donor states. Actually, the generated electrons are shifted to the MG dye and ampicillin antibiotic and upset their conjugation which leads to the disintegration of their molecules. Hole at the valence band generates hydroxyl ion via reaction with water and these ions also participate energetically in degradation of antibiotic and dye molecules. The adsorptional-photocatalytic study of ampicillin and MG degradation in the presence of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs shows that the synthesized trimetallic nanocomposite acts as a good agent for dye and ampicillin removal due to the sensitizing behaviour of CQDs. Actually, addition of CQDs lowers the band gap i.e. 1.56eV, showing high light absorbing ability. Thus, enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

The decrease in adsorption band intensities in presence of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs with irradiation time were observed for ampicillin (Fig. 6 (a and b) and for MG dye (Fig. 7 (a and b) from the above defined figures, it can be concluded that the dye and ampicillin were degraded successfully by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs. The competitive adsorptional/photocatlysis removal studies were carried out under two set of conditions as follows:

The La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs adsorbed antibiotic and dye molecules onto its surface. The extent of adsorption of antibiotic and dye by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs were studied under dark conditions. The ampicillin antibiotic and MG dye get adsorbed on the TNCs, till the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was reached further the reaction was uncovered to solar radiation for photocatalysis of test pollutants. It was recorded that only 48% MG and 88% ampicillin was adsorbed by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs till equilibrium was reached (in 1h) (Figs. 6 (c) and 7 (c)). Further photodegradation of dye and antibiotic by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs in sunlight revealed that of 96% antibiotic and 75% MG removal were recorded in 4h of treatment as shown in Table 2 .

Adsorption of dye/antibiotic
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs+Dye/Antibiotic (free in solution)↔La/Cu/Zr/CQDs-Dye/Antibiotic (adsorbed)

Photocatalysis of dye/antibiotic
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs – Dye/Antibiotic (absorbed) & Dye/Antibiotic (free in solution)+hv →La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (e− +h+) – Dye/Antibiotic
H+ +H2O→OH* +H+
h+ +OH− →OH*
e− +O2 →O2−*
O2−* or OH* +Dye and antibiotic (absorbed over La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs & free in solution)→intermediate Product→degraded product

The plot of ln Co/Ct vs. irradiation time depict a linear correlation (Figs. 6 e and 7 e) which established that the removal of antibiotic and MG dye using La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs followed pseudo first order kinetics [40].
The values of rate constant k and correlation coefficient (R2) for antibiotic and dye were given in Table 3 .

Under coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis the adsorption of antibiotic and MG dye molecules onto the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs and their photodegradation occurs at the same time. The mechanism of photodegradation is same as discussed above that TNCs contains lanthanum, copper, zirconium which generates free radicals [41]. The free radicals so produced destroy the conjugation in the adsorbed and free antibiotic or dye molecules in the solution. The difference is that the degraded antibiotic and dye molecules by-products left the surface of TNCs free for more adsorption and photocatalysis. Changes in UV–vis spectra of ampicillin and MG in visible light under coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis were given in Figs. 6 (b) and 7 (b). The anticipated mechanism for the coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis removal of ampicillin and MG were shown as:
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs+dye/Antibiotic↔La/Cu/Zr/CQDs-Dye/Antibiotic adsorbed/photocatalized (in sunlight)
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (h+)−Dye/Antibiotic adsorbed+H2O→La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (OH*)-Dye/Antibiotic+H+
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (h+)−Dye/Antibiotic adsorbed+OH− →La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (OH*)−Dye/Antibiotic
La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (e−)−Dye/Antibiotic adsorbed+O2 →La/Cu/Zr/CQDs (O2 *)−Dye/Antibiotic
O2 −* or OH+ +Dye/Antibiotic−La/Cu/Zr/CQDs→Intermediate product+La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs free for reuse

The removal percentage of ampicillin and MG dye by La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs were studied for 4h of photo irradiation and the results are given in Table 2. It has been recorded that removal of ampicillin and MG dye i.e., 91% and 86% in coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis process. In case of coupled process the adsorption of antibiotic and MG onto the photo catalyst surface and formation of electron-hole pairs on interaction with light occurs concurrently [42–44]. The adsorption of antibiotic and dye on to La/Cu/Zr/CQDs suits the progress of the photodegradation activity and enhances the degradation speed as reported in previous studies [45–47]. As absorbed antibiotic and dye molecules were easily attacked by free radicals than the dissolved antibiotic and dye molecules, the removal results are superior in this condition for MG only. The kinetics of antibiotic and dye removal depicts a linear correlation in Figs. 6 (f) and 7 (f) indicating pseudo first order kinetics. The rate constant and regression coefficients (R2) values for antibiotic and MG were given in Table 3.

In the adsorption followed by photocatalysis it has been observed that adsorption of antibiotic is higher in comparison to MG dye. Thus for antibiotic adsorption dominates the photocatalytic process hence resulting in higher antibiotic removal in adsorption followed by photocatalysis condition. However the adsorption of dye was found to be only 48% thus showing less remediation of dye in this case. Due to the possibility that photocatalyst surface is covered by antibiotic molecules (adsorption 88%) thus suppression of photocatalytic activity may occur under these conditions.

However, in coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis process, the instantaneous adsorbed amount of dye onto the photocatalyst is so small that the chances of deactivation of photo catalyst due to accumulation are decreased [26,47,48]. Therefore, photodegradation is higher and faster in case of coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis for MG [49]. The comparison of rate constants for coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis and adsorption followed photocatalysis for antibiotic and MG dye (Table 3) clearly indicates that the photocatalytic process dominates for MG dye removal under coupled conditions whereas the adsorption process dominates for antibiotic removal in adsorption followed by photocatalysis process.

The use of a simple greener method for fabrication of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs using microwave has been studied. The study obeys to the “green chemistry” principles as the synthesis takes place in aqueous solution and is cost effective. The fabrication is easy to carry out and proceeds in lesser time, as compared to previously reported techniques. The TEM and XRD analysis infer that particles are in nano dimensions. The optical band gap of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs was found to be 1.56eV. The novel La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs has an efficient adsorbing power and visible light photoactivity. The potential of La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs has been confirmed for the adsorptional/photocatalytic removal of malachite green dye and ampicillin antibiotic. The results of remediation experiments indicated that La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs exhibited a greater adsorptional behaviour for antibiotic and photocatalytic performance for MG dye. The results obtained are very promising in sight of different adsorptional/photocatalytic applications of the La/Cu/Zr/CQDs TNCs for the remediation of rising contaminants such pharmaceuticals by products, pesticides and chlorophenols etc. 96% ampicillin antibiotic and 86% of malachite green dye was degraded in 4h of photoirridation under adsorption in dark followed by photocatalysis and coupled adsorptional/photocatalysis process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Shoolni University, Solan. This work was supported by the Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University for funding through Vice Deanship of Scientific Research Chairs.
